section.title: Eliphaz's Third Speech
section.sectionRationale: This is Eliphaz's last word, and it is his harshest. In the first cycle he was gentle, in the second he grew sharper, and now, unable to answer Job's arguments, he abandons restraint altogether and simply invents a list of crimes against him. It is the clearest sign in the whole book that a theology which cannot explain the evidence in front of it will eventually manufacture evidence to fit the theology.
section.thesis: When a fixed doctrine of retribution meets a reality it cannot account for, it does not bend, it accuses; Eliphaz wraps true words about God's greatness and the call to reconcile around fabricated charges he has no grounds to bring against an innocent man.
section.frameNote: Four movements: Eliphaz's opening question of whether a man's righteousness could ever benefit God; his invented list of Job's supposed crimes against the vulnerable; a theology of God's transcendence twisted to excuse presumption; and a closing appeal to reconcile and be restored that is true in general yet wrongly aimed at Job.

verses.text: Then Eliphaz the Temanite replied:
“Can a man be of use to God? Can even a wise man benefit Him?
Does it delight the {{shaddai|Almighty}} that you are righteous? Does He profit if your ways are blameless?
Is it for your reverence that He {{yakach|rebukes}} you and enters into judgment against you?
Is not your wickedness great? Are not your iniquities endless?
For you needlessly demanded security from your brothers and deprived the naked of their clothing.
You gave no water to the weary and withheld food from the famished,
while the land belonged to a mighty man, and a man of honor lived on it.
You sent widows away empty-handed, and the strength of the fatherless was crushed.
Therefore snares surround you, and sudden peril terrifies you;
it is so dark you cannot see, and a flood of water covers you.

units.label: A theology of distance twisted
units.note: God is so high He cannot see, they say

verses.text: Is not God as high as the heavens? Look at the highest stars, how lofty they are!
Yet you say: ‘What does God know? Does He judge through thick darkness?
Thick clouds veil Him so He does not see us as He traverses the vault of heaven.’
Will you stay on the ancient path that wicked men have trod?
They were snatched away before their time, and their foundations were swept away by a flood.
They said to God, ‘Depart from us. What can the Almighty do to us?’
But it was He who filled their houses with good things; so I stay far from the counsel of the wicked.
The righteous see it and are glad; the innocent mock them:
‘Surely our foes are destroyed, and fire has consumed their excess.’
Reconcile now and be at {{shalom|peace}} with Him; thereby good will come to you.
Receive instruction from His mouth, and lay up His words in your heart.
If you return to the Almighty, you will be restored. If you remove injustice from your tents
and consign your gold to the dust and the gold of Ophir to the stones of the ravines,
then the Almighty will be your gold and the finest silver for you.
Surely then you will delight in the Almighty and lift up your face to God.
You will pray to Him, and He will hear you, and you will fulfill your vows.
Your decisions will be carried out, and {{or|light}} will shine on your ways.
When men are brought low and you say, ‘Lift them up!’ then He will save the lowly.
He will deliver even one who is not innocent, rescuing him through the cleanness of your hands.”

shaddai.gloss: The Almighty, a divine title used throughout Job more than anywhere else in the Old Testament. It stresses overwhelming power, which is exactly what Eliphaz appeals to: surely such a God has no need of, and takes no note of, one man's righteousness or sin.

yakach.gloss: To rebuke, reason, or argue a case, often in a legal setting. Eliphaz asks whether God enters this kind of courtroom dispute with Job over his reverence; the same root recurs across Job for the case Job wants to argue before God, 13:3, 23:7.

shalom.gloss: Peace, wholeness, well-being. Eliphaz's call to 'be at peace with Him' is true wisdom in itself, echoed throughout Scripture, but here it is aimed at a man who has done nothing to be reconciled from.

or.gloss: Light. Eliphaz promises Job that light will shine on his ways if he repents, the same word used of the light God speaks into darkness, and the very thing Job feels he has lost, 19:8, 23:17.

basic.context: This is Eliphaz's third and final speech, and it is a shock. In his first two speeches he spoke in generalities about sin and suffering. Now, unable to answer Job's case, he simply invents specific crimes: robbing the naked, starving the hungry, crushing widows and orphans. Nothing in the story supports any of this. He is not reporting facts; he is forcing Job to fit his theology.
basic.hermeneutics: The chapter is honest about how good theology curdles into cruelty when it must be defended at all costs. Eliphaz's opening question and his closing appeal both contain real truth, God's self sufficiency, the goodness of returning to Him, but the middle section shows a man so committed to his system that he will lie to keep it standing. Job later swears under oath that these very charges are false, 31:16-22.
basic.originalLanguages: Eliphaz invokes Shaddai, the Almighty, a title that dominates Job, to argue that God is too great to be affected by human righteousness or sin, and therefore Job's suffering must be punishment for real crimes. He uses yakach, the courtroom word for arguing a case, the same word Job has used to long for his day in court. And he promises Job that or, light, will return if he repents, unaware that Job already walks in a darkness no repentance from invented sin could lift.
basic.typology: Job stands here as a righteous man falsely accused by those who claim to speak for God, a pattern that reaches its fullest form in Jesus, accused of blasphemy and sedition by the very religious leaders who should have recognized Him. The false friend who wounds in the name of truth anticipates every accuser who uses God's name to justify cruelty.
basic.christ: Eliphaz's fabricated charges against an innocent man foreshadow the false testimony brought against Jesus, who also had done nothing to deserve His suffering and was condemned by men certain they were serving God. Job's silence under false accusation, and his eventual vindication in chapter 42, points toward the greater silence and greater vindication of Christ.
basic.god: God does not need Job's righteousness, Eliphaz is right about that, yet God is not distant or blind as Eliphaz's fabricated evidence and the friends' whole system implies. The book will show that God sees, cares, and eventually rebukes Eliphaz himself for these very words, 42:7.
basic.crossReferences: Job 31:16-22 has Job deny under oath every charge Eliphaz invents here. Psalm 34:14 and Proverbs 3:7 echo the true counsel buried in verses 21-27. Matthew 26:59-61 shows false witnesses inventing charges against Jesus much as Eliphaz invents them against Job.
basic.oneStory: Eliphaz's speech is theology weaponized: true things about God's greatness and the goodness of returning to Him, wrapped around lies invented to protect a broken system. The Bible's answer to this kind of religious cruelty is Christ, falsely accused and finally vindicated, who exposes every attempt to use God's name to crush the innocent.

context.literary: The last speech in the third cycle from Eliphaz, and the sharpest turn in tone across all three of his speeches. It moves from a rhetorical question about God's self sufficiency, into fabricated accusations, into a meditation on God's transcendence, and closes with a call to repent that echoes true wisdom while missing Job's actual situation entirely.
context.historical: Ancient Near Eastern wisdom generally assumed a tight moral order: righteousness brings blessing, wickedness brings ruin. Eliphaz represents this tradition at its most rigid, so committed to the formula that when the facts run out, he supplies new ones.
context.canonical: This chapter exposes the failure point of retribution theology carried to its extreme, preparing the reader for both Job's continued protest and God's eventual rebuke of the three friends by name in 42:7-8.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show a once measured friend reduced to fabrication when his theology cannot survive contact with reality, exposing both the danger of a rigid doctrine of retribution and the cruelty it can produce even from someone who believes he is defending God.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes Eliphaz's failure; it does not prescribe his methods. The enduring truths, God's self sufficiency and the goodness of returning to Him, are prescriptive and true in themselves, even though Eliphaz misapplies them to a man who has done nothing wrong.

debates.question: Are Eliphaz's accusations in 22:6-9 based on any real knowledge of Job's life?

views.label: Speculative fabrication
views.case: Nothing in the narrative supports these charges, and Job explicitly denies each of them under oath in chapter 31; most readers conclude Eliphaz invented them to fit his theology.

views.label: Exaggerated community rumor
views.case: Some suggest Eliphaz may be inflating vague grumbling or envy from Job's former community rather than inventing from nothing, though the text gives no support for even this.

debates.bearing: Either way, God explicitly says in 42:7 that Eliphaz did not speak rightly about Him, confirming the charges were groundless.

debates.question: Does the true counsel in 22:21-30 still carry weight, given that it is wrongly applied?

views.label: Discount it entirely
views.case: Since it is delivered as an accusation against an innocent man, some treat the whole appeal as tainted and untrustworthy.

views.label: True wisdom, wrongly aimed
views.case: The call to treasure God's words and find your gold in Him is echoed elsewhere in Scripture as good counsel in itself; the failure is not in the content but in assuming Job needs to repent of sins he never committed.

debates.bearing: Scripture can contain genuinely true statements in the mouths of flawed or even condemned speakers; the reader must judge the application, not just the words.

study.originalLanguages: Eliphaz leans on Shaddai, the Almighty, the great divine title of this book, to argue God is unaffected by human conduct, so Job's suffering must answer to real crimes. He uses yakach, the language of legal argument, the very word for the case Job longs to bring before God. And he dangles or, light, as the reward of repentance, not knowing Job already walks in a darkness no confession of invented sin could dispel.

typology.type: The righteous man falsely accused by those claiming to speak for God, Job 22:6-9
typology.fulfillment: Jesus, accused of blasphemy by the religious leaders who should have known Him, Matthew 26:59-66.

study.christ: Eliphaz manufactures crimes against a righteous man in the name of defending God's justice, a pattern the Gospels record in full at the trial of Jesus, where false witnesses were sought out because the true charge, that He had done nothing wrong, would convict no one. Job's silence before false accusation and his later vindication by God Himself, 42:7, anticipate the far greater silence and vindication of Christ, who was condemned though innocent and raised to prove it.
study.god: God does not need human righteousness to be complete, as Eliphaz rightly says, but He is not distant, blind, or indifferent to false accusation as Eliphaz's fabricated evidence implies. The narrator and God Himself will side with Job's honesty over Eliphaz's tidy theology, 42:7-8.

crossReferences.note: Job answers under solemn oath the very charges Eliphaz invents here, denying each one.
God rebukes Eliphaz by name for not having spoken of Him what is right, confirming this speech's failure.
Do not be wise in your own eyes, echoing the true counsel buried within Eliphaz's flawed appeal.
False witnesses invent charges against Jesus, a pattern of fabricated accusation against the innocent.

study.oneStory: Eliphaz's last speech shows what happens when a rigid theology meets a reality it cannot explain: it invents evidence rather than admit its limits. The Bible's larger story exposes and answers this cruelty in Christ, the truly innocent man falsely accused in God's name, whose vindication by resurrection undoes every false verdict passed against the righteous.

sermon.title: Job: Wrestling with Suffering
sermon.why: Piper's sermon on Job 2:11-31:40 walks through all three speech cycles and specifically quotes Eliphaz's final speech in chapter 22, showing how 'this is so preposterous' that Bildad and Zophar can barely continue after it, tracing the collapse of the friends' theology of retribution.

quiz.q: What is the basic problem with Eliphaz's accusations in 22:6-9?

options.text: They are invented charges with no support in the story, later denied by Job under oath.
options.note: Job answers each of these very charges in chapter 31, and God rebukes Eliphaz's speech in 42:7.

options.text: They are somewhat exaggerated but based on real faults in Job.
options.note: The text gives no basis for any of these charges; they are not exaggerations of real faults.

options.text: They are general warnings, not meant as specific accusations.
options.note: The language is specific and personal, aimed squarely at Job, not general.

options.text: They are proven true later in the book.
options.note: The opposite happens; God vindicates Job and rebukes Eliphaz.

quiz.q: Why does Eliphaz's opening question in 22:2-3 matter?

options.text: It rightly notes God's self sufficiency, then wrongly uses that truth to conclude Job's suffering must be deserved.
options.note: The premise is true, God needs nothing from us, but the conclusion drawn from it does not follow.

options.text: It proves God is indifferent to human righteousness altogether.
options.note: God's self sufficiency does not mean indifference; the book shows God deeply engaged with Job.

options.text: It is simply false and has no theological value.
options.note: The premise about God's self sufficiency is itself sound biblical teaching.

options.text: It shows Eliphaz has abandoned belief in God.
options.note: Eliphaz remains a fervent believer; his error is application, not unbelief.

quiz.q: What does God ultimately say about Eliphaz's speeches, according to 42:7?

options.text: Eliphaz and his two friends had not spoken of God what is right, as Job had.
options.note: This is God's direct verdict, requiring the friends to be reconciled through Job's intercession.

options.text: Eliphaz spoke perfectly and only Job needed correction.
options.note: The opposite is stated; Eliphaz is the one rebuked.

options.text: Both Job and Eliphaz were equally wrong.
options.note: God singles out the three friends for correction, not Job.

options.text: God rebukes the three friends by name, though Job too was corrected for presumption earlier in the whirlwind speeches.
options.note: True that Job was humbled earlier, but 42:7 itself is a direct, positive verdict that Job specifically spoke rightly about God.

quiz.q: How should the true counsel in 22:21-27, reconcile with God and treasure His words, be read?

options.text: As genuine wisdom in itself, misapplied to a man who had no sin of this kind to repent of.
options.note: Scripture can carry true statements in a flawed speech; the failure here is application, not content.

options.text: As proof that Job really was living in unrepented sin.
options.note: The narrative and God's own verdict contradict this reading.

options.text: As worthless because Eliphaz said it.
options.note: The content itself echoes true wisdom found elsewhere in Scripture, even though misapplied.

options.text: As evidence Eliphaz secretly did not believe in God.
options.note: Eliphaz is a sincere believer whose theology has simply failed him here.

quiz.q: What does this chapter reveal about the danger of a rigid doctrine of retribution?

options.text: When it cannot explain the evidence, it will eventually manufacture evidence rather than be revised.
options.note: Eliphaz's invented charges are the clearest example in the book of this danger.

options.text: It always leads people away from belief in God.
options.note: Eliphaz remains devout throughout; the danger is cruelty, not unbelief.

options.text: It has no real danger since it is usually accurate.
options.note: The book systematically shows this doctrine fails to explain Job's case.

options.text: It mainly makes people speak more harshly, though it need not lead all the way to fabricating charges.
options.note: Eliphaz's case shows the danger can indeed escalate all the way to invented accusations, not just a harsher tone.

questions: Eliphaz began gently and ended by inventing crimes to defend his theology. Where might you be tempted to twist facts, or assume the worst about someone, to keep a tidy explanation intact?
Verses 21-27 contain real wisdom, wrongly aimed at an innocent man. How can you learn to receive true counsel without assuming every hard word about you must be deserved?
God later says Eliphaz did not speak rightly about Him, even though Eliphaz was sincere and articulate. What does this teach you about the difference between sounding right and being right?
Job was falsely accused in God's name by someone who believed he was helping. Have you ever been on the receiving end of that, or been tempted to do it to someone else?
Job's vindication came only in chapter 42, long after this speech. Where are you waiting for vindication now, and how does trusting God's justice change how you wait?

translationNotes.note: 'Be of use' renders a term for profit or benefit; Eliphaz's question assumes a transactional view of righteousness that the whole book will complicate.
The quoted words, 'What does God know, does He judge through thick darkness', are Eliphaz's caricature of what he imagines wicked men say, not anything Job has actually said.

These closing verses are notoriously difficult in Hebrew; the BSB rendering follows the sense that God saves the humble and even delivers the guilty through the intercession of a righteous man, a theme that anticipates Job's own intercessory role for his friends in 42:8-9.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Job 22:1-30. No em or en dashes used anywhere in this file.
